Figure 17: Case study: Gantt chart and visualization results. Tasks’ details in Fig. 8, and environment’s details in Fig. 7.
Figure 5: SAP: Spatial aware by path planning for low-level agent.

Figure 12: Visualized results of Table 6. Makespan under varying numbers of humans and robots.
Figure 13: Visualization of Table 7. Makespan in unseen product quantities.
Figure 7: Production workspace including humans, robots, machines, and materials.

Table 8 Makespan and distance performance in the testing phase for EBQ-N combined with various low-level algorithms. Note that a "†" indicates the performance of our proposed algorithm. "Hn" denotes the number of humans is set to n, while "Rn" indicates n robots during testing.

Table 7 The table presents zero-shot results, evaluating algorithms on unseen product quantities post-training. "NumN" means producing N products. The performance of our proposed model is marked with a “†".
Figure 14: The improvement degree of EBQ-∗ Vs. D3QN and others, D3QN is the baseline.
Figure 11: Evaluation of high-level algorithms during training, all use SAP as the low-level algorithm.
Figure 4: Network architecture for high-level agent, including feature embedding, transformer architecture, dueling network and Noisy Net.

Figure 15: Showcase of path planning for offline node graph generation. For both humans and robots, we present two cases each, using diverse start nodes from task-related working area nodes. Path results are provided from each start node to all other task-related working area nodes.
Figure 16: Evaluation curves during training intervals for EBQ-N combined with various low-level algorithms.
Figure 1: Illustration of the real-time production process flow and state information.

Figure 2: Our proposed hierarchical human-robot TPA algorithm with the high-level agent for real-time task planning, and a low-level agent for task allocation. The details of the high-level agent and low-level agent are expressed in Sec. 3.3 and Sec. 3.5.
Figure 9: Evaluation curves during training intervals for efficient buffer mechanism.

Table 6 Makespan performance in the testing phase. Note that a "†" indicates the performance of our proposed algorithm. "Hn" denotes the number of humans is set to n, while "Rn" indicates n robots during testing.
Figure 10: Training curves of high-level algorithms, all use SAP as the low-level algorithm.
